title: JK Flip-Flop (NAND Gate Implementation)
aliases: j k flip flop gate level
examTip: JK truth table: J=0,K=0=No Change | J=0,K=1=Reset(Q=0) | J=1,K=0=Set(Q=1) | J=1,K=1=Toggle(Q-complement). The Toggle state (J=K=1) is the unique feature distinguishing JK from SR. Characteristic equation: Q(next) = J·Q̄ + K̄·Q.
theory:
The JK Flip-Flop is the most versatile and universal sequential logic element, resolving the undefined/forbidden state of the SR Flip-Flop (where S=R=1 was prohibited) by introducing the Toggle operation when both J=1 and K=1. Every other flip-flop type can be derived from the JK flip-flop through appropriate input conditioning.

NAND Gate Implementation: This implementation uses two 3-input NAND gates (G1, G2) for the first stage (input logic) and two 2-input NAND gates (G3, G4) for the cross-coupled SR latch output stage. G1 computes S-bar = NAND(J, CLK, Q-bar) and G2 computes R-bar = NAND(K, CLK, Q). The critical difference from the SR flip-flop is the feedback: the Q output is fed back to G2's input (to allow K to reset the FF only when Q=1), and Q-bar is fed back to G1's input (to allow J to set the FF only when Q=0).

Feedback Mechanism and Race Around Problem: The feedback connections from the output latch back to G1 and G2 are what give the JK its toggle behavior. When J=K=1 and CLK=1: if Q=0, then Q-bar=1, so G1's output goes LOW (setting Q=1), and then Q-bar=0, making G2's output go LOW, toggling Q back to 0 — this creates the 'race-around condition' in level-triggered JK FFs. This is why practical JK flip-flops are edge-triggered or use master-slave configurations.

Truth Table: J=0, K=0 → No Change (Q retains previous value); J=0, K=1 → Reset (Q=0); J=1, K=0 → Set (Q=1); J=1, K=1 → Toggle (Q=Q-bar). The characteristic equation is Q(next) = J·Q-bar + K-bar·Q.

Universality: The JK FF can function as an SR FF (J=S, K=R), a D FF (K=J-bar, D=J), or a T FF (J=K=T). This makes it the most flexible building block in sequential circuit design.
keyPoints: Characteristic equation: Q(next) = J·Q̄ + K̄·Q — resolves SR FF's forbidden state
Toggle state (J=K=1) is unique to JK FF — Q complements on every active clock edge
Three-input NAND gates incorporate Q and Q̄ feedback to prevent forbidden state
Race-around condition occurs in level-triggered JK FFs when J=K=1 and CLK stays HIGH
Edge-triggered or master-slave design solves the race-around problem
JK FF is universal: can be configured as SR, D, or T flip-flop
74LS76 is the standard TTL dual JK flip-flop IC
useCases: Binary ripple counters and synchronous counters using Toggle mode (J=K=1)
Frequency dividers — a JK FF in toggle mode divides input frequency by 2
State machine design where both Set, Reset, Hold, and Toggle behaviors are needed
